item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The second image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as it comes from here and is All Rights Reserved. Thus, the image has been removed. The second image comes from here and is cc-compliant.

item.title: Why is the 1950's russian man worried that he's sounding like a communist?
item.content:encoded: I feel like that could have been interesting if it where developed more, but now it just seems confusing

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You never refined that "people are teleported and revived" thingie you mentioned. But the idea is cool regardless so I'll upvote it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"SCP-2187-B is geostationary, meaning it retains a constant position relative to the Earth's surface, despite that is much farther from the Earth than would allow so."

That sentence just come across as awkward, and I don't know how to fix it without restructuring it entirely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Soviet project which worships the Atom, and uses large amounts of nuclear or energy based weapons? Is this in any way related to SCP-2675?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "The Power of Atom!" I thought of Fallout 3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"The Power of Atom!"
How can you not compair it to BioShock?
The only thing its missing is the phrase "would you kindly…"

Im stumped on this one.
To much…science.
Thats not to say i didnt like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just assumed they only sent 4 foundation personnel in (because they're expensive) and sent lots of d-class

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Caucasian is scientific, just commonly argued whether it's the correct term.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sci-finess at its sci-finest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ha, if there is anything I really liked about the skip, it's gotta be the sci-finess at its finest. Space stations, teleportation, "chosen race", with a hints of Bioshock. But then again, you probably had me at the building filled with suddenly teleported dead bodies.

I would very much like to see this on the mainlist. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hm… I enjoyed some of it, enough to not downvote. it's certainly well written and has a few good details. But overall it doesn't really work for me — I don't find it interesting enough, at least off the bat…

No vote for now. I'll have to think about it.

Nitpicks:

  • I'm bugged by using 'amnesiac' instead of 'amnestic'.
  • "Caucasian" isn't scientific; I'd get whatever necessary info out of the way in another fashion (blonde man of European descent, or something).

You can get away with both those things quite easily, so take that FWIW

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had the same reaction as Lumancer, but given there are native Esperanto speakers, I suppose it's possible. I just have no idea what one would sound like.

Anyway, I feel like I saw an early and very different version of this in the chat, and I like what you did with it a lot more now. It's like a 50's sci-fi meets the scales of Anubis, mixed with Esperanto propaganda. Totally off the wall while having internal consistency.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm. I'm not totally sure, actually. That makes me reconsider.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aren't accents region dependent, not language dependent? I'm not sure that it's possible to have an Esperanto accent, is my point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, right. Yeah, I made a bad call and tried to translate them myself, when I probably should have gotten someone who actually speaks Russian. I'll make those edits, if you think they're appropriate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Also gratuitous Russian.

Sorry, I should've been clearer there, but I was talking in trope-speach. What I meant was not that bits in Russian were out of place, but that they were written by someone who doesn't speak Russian very well. I don't know if that was intentional or not; if it was, it strained my disbelief and I don't like it.

Specifically, "Питер" is not a person's name; if anything, it's a short name for "Санкт-Петербург", a city in Russia. I think what you wanted was "Пётр", as you mention Piotr in the supplement. Also in the picture in the supplement, the Russian part should be either "Новое Суждение" or "Новый Суд"; the later is probably more appropriate, as "суждение" is likely to refer to a person's judgment over some matter, not to judgment conduct on a person.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Err, teleport and revive. The humans were alive upon arrival. I'll try to make it fit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't like things that judge people and then send them to heaven/hell/nonexistence, or otherwise punish them for their sins.

Do we have a lot of these? I didn't realize. Oh well. Can you provide some examples I can compare with?

This also suffers from execution issues. The description is extremely convoluted and hard to read, describing all the unimportant details while leaving the core effects either implied or hidden in details.

Yeah, this is just because, well, I'm bad at writing. I do realize the main description needs some effort, and I might try to fix it. I tried to go for subtly at some points, but subtly in the midst of pointless is bad.

Also I'm still not clear on how the whole thing works. A person's consciousness is transferred into a ticker, which is then inserted into the elevator and sent elsewhere? What happens to the body? What happens to the bodyless consciousness once it arrives? Why can't it be a single machine instead of two, why does it have to be so convoluted?

People arrive with their body. They then get scanned, get a ticket, and move into an elevator. It could have been a single machine, but the elevator is completely unnecessary to begin with. I decided to write it this way as a thematic decision, but I understand it's cluttered.

We don't know what's in SCP-2000-B. That's the point.

The Foundation is 5% more evil than D-Class. I groaned at that part.

Cliche, right? That was a throw-away line. Well, they aren't necessarily 5% more evil, just 5% more evil by the creators standards. If other people hate it, I'll take it out.

Also gratuitous Russian.

This is one thing you're definitely wrong about. This was a secret Russian-American project. They were planning on welcoming every person who died, and those were the two major powers at the time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: hey, i liked it. crazy transhuman esperanto cult-thingy. there were tonal issues though, so it's a somewhat hesitant upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Something unclear for me there:

It is currently theorized that equipment inside SCP-2000-A was able to teleport humans inside upon their death, and that SCP-2000-A was somehow activated, summoning hundreds of people over the course of three minutes. Although the building could accommodate a maximum of a person a second (much faster than necessary at the time of its design), the higher death rate coupled with the confusion of arrivals lead to a build up, as no one was able to escape through the building's reenforced exits.

So… if it teleports people upon their death, how come they were apparently still alive (otherwise it would hardly be unusual that none of them was able to leave)?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, those paticular Foundation employees are 5% more evil than that selection of Class-Ds.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't like things that judge people and then send them to heaven/hell/nonexistence, or otherwise punish them for their sins.

This also suffers from execution issues. The description is extremely convoluted and hard to read, describing all the unimportant details while leaving the core effects either implied or hidden in details.

Also I'm still not clear on how the whole thing works. A person's consciousness is transferred into a ticker, which is then inserted into the elevator and sent elsewhere? What happens to the body? What happens to the bodyless consciousness once it arrives? Why can't it be a single machine instead of two, why does it have to be so convoluted?

The Foundation is 5% more evil than D-Class. I groaned at that part.

Also gratuitous Russian.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, yeah. I can't really respond but that, yeah, I did my best. Second SCP. I probably wouldn't have even tried writing this yet, if it weren't for the contest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Typo.

Eh, I tried. It's overall sort of rushed, so I might not put it up immediately when the contest ends. You read the supplement, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Each scanner is a hollow acrylic hemisphere with lined with a complex array of metal instruments.

Typo?

Like the idea here, but in the end I wasn't left with much feeling or contemplation. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, I haven't played Bioshock, but even I knew that this is heavily reminiscent of it. That's not the reason I downvoted, but it didn't make me favorably inclined towards the article, either.

Points in favor of this one is that it's an ambitious effort and there's obviously a cohesive story that you want to tell. I respect that and the attempt to build something of complexity.

Weighing that down, however, is how convoluted this article was as I read it. I feel like there wasn't enough in the first part of the article to set me up for what was coming, and as a result the front end feels disjointed and unnecessarily complicated. Later on you drop some hints, but I feel like I've slogged to get there. The supplemental section is a little scattered, though I acknowledge how difficult that style of exposition can be. It just felt a little…random, I guess. Also, I find it hard to believe that no one remembered to bring food to the New Utopia Paradise. A little too far from reality and too close to a Bob the Angry Flower comic.

This is a valiant attempt, but ultimately this was just too clunky in the execution for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How did I lose you? Do you mean you don't understand why that phrase is there? Because really, that's the sentence that ties it all together.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"over 70% of D-Class and 75% of Foundation personnel have caused SCP-2000-A-2 to descend. D-Class whose scans have caused SCP-2000-A-2 to ascend generally express deep regret or religious repentance over past immoral actions, but are in a sound state of mind."

Sorry, you lost me there. =/

The rest is too good for me to downvote, but I don't know yet if I should upvote in good conscience.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Soulless why don't you like my stuff :(

But yeah, it's a LOT like Bioshock. I wasn't sure exactly how to curb that, but I went for more Atompunk. Curious, what about this would you say "isn't really your thing"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't help but compare a lot of this to Bioshock, except less immersive because it's hard o get more immersive than Bioshock. Other than that, it's not really my thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Certainly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can you please change "equipment typical of the 1950's" to "1950's era equipment"? It's bugging me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm glad! I didn't mean to make it overly trope-y, but I see what you mean.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this take on… well, all of the sci-fi tropes that you kind of seamlessly meshed together here to make this happen. I'm liking this more than I thought I would. Super creative. Enjoy your +1.
